And by reason that Christ is Go[illegible] therefore is he head of the church, ministering to the body li[illegible] spirit, wisdom, and all gifts celestial. Epheſ. v. Ca[illegible] Pope of Rome, that most wicked man of sin doe th[illegible] What an head is he than? without life, without brain[illegible] most folyſhe. As he is described in the xi. of Zacha. And is a shame, that we will not se these things, being blind ever. Christ is everlasting, omnipotent, and that knows thing: And he may be the health and head of the body. I[illegible] in the beginning, saith he, was the word, and the word [illegible] with God. &c. Christ him self: before Abraham was, saith [illegible] I am. Therefore the Heretics lye, deniyng Christ to be[illegible] ry God, of the same substance with the father. He is the [illegible] dome of God, all things are subject to him. Ephe. the [illegible] And he him self fulfills all thing, after the same sort be[illegible] present with his church.

[note 5.12]Now are his eyes also not darkened nor blind, but fie[illegible] and bright. For Christ knows all thing. Christ's eye[illegible] watchful, nothing is hyd from him, he sees all things [illegible] are done, both good and evil. And he sees to the intent may judge and require. He is light in darkness, and the si[illegible] of Christ is to good men joyful in perils. Finally the judgements of Christ are rightuous. The Prophet David: The eyes of the Lord, saith he, upon the just, and his ears are to their prayer. Again: The face of the Lord is on them that do evil. And like as the head is not plucked from the body, so Christ can not be absent from his church. And saying that his eyes are quick sighted, and that the Lord forſeeth all our things, and hath the charge over us, how is he absent from his church? What needs there any vicar?

And the fete of the Lord are of copper,[note 5.13] or like unto brass and frankincense burning in a fornace. For Chalcolibaum, is a word compounded of brass and frankincense.[note 5.14] This notes Eraſmus, and that Swidas shows also the same, that there is a kind of Copper more precious than gold: which he saith is made of Salt peter and of a stone. Plin. in the xxxiiii. and ii. Chapter. calls it a kind of brass, which is dug out of the veins of the earth, in times paste had in price. It should seem to me to be the same, which in the first and tenth of Ezekiel is called Haſmal, a present remedy against poisons. For if wine intoxicated be put into a cup thereof, it will hiss. And so is the death and poison detected.

The most clean brazen and fiery fete, do signify the conversation, and the ways of the Lord blameles, his judgements right and just. And that he so walk in the church, and governs all things, that in the mean season all vncleanes be detec [illegible]ed and consumed, but he him self remains always most holy and pure. For fire pourgeth. God is a consuming fire.

But the voice of Christ,[note 5.15] is as it were the noise of many waters, not so much for that all nations and people do commend and praise him: But for as much as the Gospel and word of God, came into the whole world. Which voice also most mighty kings could less assuage and appease, than they could do the guſſhing of waters,[note 5.16] or to stop the wyndes in ſackes. Therefore by the power of preaching the Lord is always present in his church.

The hand is an instrument of all instruments, especially the righthande.[note 5.17] In this Christ holds seven stars, to wit seven prelates or pastors of churches in Asia: And even all the bishops throughout the whole world Christ by his power gives to us as Pastors, and instructs, comfort[illegible] confirms and defends them, to the end they should preac[illegible] his word. Whereby he may join him self to the churc[illegible] Christ works by them in the church and preserves th[illegible]

[note 5.18]The same is more lively expressed in the words that f[illegible] lowe. For a sharpe two edged sword comes out of [illegible] Lords mouth. This swore, is the word of God, as [illegible] right well declared in the sixth to the Epheſ. and fourth to [illegible] Hebrews. And this word or sword hangs not upon [illegible] wall nor sticks fast in the sheathe, nor hangs by the f[illegible] but comes out of the mouth. He saith not, it came foort[illegible] it shall come forth: But it comes forth, as the thing th[illegible] in continual operation, or perpetual preaching through[illegible] the world. And it is two edged, sharpe and pearſing, aſ[illegible] in the heart of the Godly unto salvation, as in the heart[illegible] the wicked to payne and condemnation. And yet at [illegible] day comes out that sword of the mouth of Christ by mouths of Ministers. The word of Christ is in deed [illegible] temned of the world, and is called of many a fable: But [illegible] a sword, and that a sword out of Christ's mouth. All [illegible] unfaithful do find and shall find this, howe soever [illegible] resist. With this sword Christ kills the wicked. And effect of this sword is greater than was the sword of A[illegible] ander, Pompey, Iulius Caesar, or Marius, Attille, or [illegible] merlane. Neither makes it any ſkylle, though the w[illegible] now acknowledge it not. It shall doe in time to com[illegible] their greatest evil. Doubtless with this spirit of his m[illegible] the Lord continues always to comfort and govern[illegible] church, so that he is never absent from the same.

Finally the countenance of Christ shines as the [illegible] doth in his greatest strength about none, when it is [illegible] bright, clear, and pleasant, by the countenance we [illegible] men chiefly. Therefore by the countenance we know C[illegible] The countenance of Christ is light. Christ therefore is [illegible] And that verily a divine and eternal light, lyghtyng a[illegible] that they may also be made the children of light: and than[illegible] faces of saints may shine in that day, as bright as the si[illegible] and as the face of Christ shone. Math. 13. and.17. And f[illegible] he doth thus communicate this light unto us. (John [illegible] and.1. John. 1.) How is it to be thought that Christ should be absent from his church? Thou see how he is present.

And so hath our Lord Christ exhibited him self to us to be scene unto salvation, and hath opened him self wholly unto us, as he is, what he doth for us, and howe he is in his church. In these things are all the mysteries of the Gospel comprehended. For what can you say of Christ, that you have not herein compryſed. Let us therefore remember them. and write them in our minds, that we may embrace Christ king and bishop, and that we never let him depart out of our arms. To him be glory.

## ¶ How John was affected towards the vision to him exhibited, the comfort of John, and the exposition of the vision, applied unto consolation. The sixth Sermon.

ANd when I saw him I fell at his fete even as dead: And he laid his right hand upon me, saying unto me: Fear not, I am the first and the last, and am alive, and was [illegible]ead. And behold I am alive for ever more, [illegible]nd have the keys of hell, and of death, write [illegible]herfore the things which thou haste scene, [illegible]nd the things which are, and the things which shall be fulfilled hereafter: And the mystery of the seven Stars which thou saw in my right hand, and the seven golden candelſtickes. The seven Stars are the messengers of the seven congregations: And [illegible]he seven candelſtickes which thou saw, [illegible]re the seven congregations.

It follows how blessed S. John was moved with that celestial and wonderful vision: And how he received consolation, more over the exposition of the vision applied to his comfort, with a commandment to indite all these thing[illegible] diligently.

[note 6.1]What time he had fully scene this divine and heavenly sight of Christ our Lord, sitting on the right hand of God i[illegible] glory, his strength failing him, he falls down on the earth: and lying at the fete of the Lord, is like a dead body. W[illegible] read that the same chanced to Daniel in the x. Chapter. And other men of God also have been feared with the visions o[illegible] Angels. The women also in the new Testament tremble[illegible] at the sepulchre, seeing Angels. Peter was amassed at th[illegible] greatness of the miracle. Luke v. And falling at the knees [illegible] the Lord, cries out, go from me, Lord, for I am a ſynful m[illegible] For Godly visions bewray our infirmity:[note 6.2] Neither be [illegible] apt or sufficiently pourged, to behold those ſuperceleſt[illegible] things with eyes and minds sick and not yet well pu[illegible] fied. Therefore must the elect be glorified in an other life, th[illegible] they may be made partakers of the glory celestial. In th[illegible] mean season here all godly are humbled and abased by hyg[illegible] visions and revelations.[note 6.3] For they advance not them ſelue[illegible] proudly through the glory of reuelatio: But perceiving th[illegible] natural corruption, they crave pardon, and the augmentation of the ſuperceleſtiall grace and light. For unless we b[illegible] illumined with the spirit of God, we shall lye like dead folk[illegible] how so ever we receive with our corporal senses the viſio[illegible] celestial.

[note 6.4]But they that humble them selves before the Lord, find[illegible] a most present consolation at the Lords hand. Wherfo[illegible] there came to S. John immediately both in word and de[illegible] a full consolation. For the Angel representing the person[illegible] of Christ, lays his right hand upon John: The which is token of amity, protection, and of present help. For in [illegible] pressing this manner of speaking in dutche, we say therefore [illegible] laying on the hand is signified, that Christ is good to John, ready to help him. Which incontinently he makes play[illegible] by the addition of words, saying, fear not.[note 6.5] Which ſaiy[illegible] is common every where in the story of the Gospel: And th[illegible] fore is most gospel like, that is to say most lucky. For God commands the humbled to be of good hope, and to live assured under the protectio of the highest. Which verily we understand to be spoken not to John alone but to all us also, that we in like manner, albeit that we feel the infirmity of our flesh, should yet hope well of the goodness and mercy of God. Here follows that cause more fully declared, why John should not be afraid. For the vision showed was not exhibited for the terror of him: but that John might perceive how great and mighty he is which is prepared for the defence of him and all the faithful. As though he should say. Where thou see how great he is, which hath taken upon him to defend thee, who finally protects and governs the whole Church, there is no cause why thou shouldest be afraid. But rather execute boldly, that he comaundeth thee. Write that he co maunds to be written. Be not afraid of men, fear God rather. For if good me be so sore afraid at the sight of him, where shall the enemies and contemners of God appear?

Therefore consequently he expoudeth the vision, teaching who he is, which was scene like to the son of man walking among the golden Candelſtickes. And he applies this expoſitio unto comfort: that both John and every faithful may perceive, how mighty Christ is, and what the faithful have by him obtained. For the Angel tempers his speech so, that we may seem to hear all things spoke to us not by the mouth of the Angel, but of Christ him self.[note 6.6] And this exposition hath his parts. For first he declares (as I said even now) whose Image it was that was showed. Than is annexed a commandment to write this book. After that is opened the mystery of the stars. Finally the ſecretnes of the cadelſticks is revealed: And all these things right plainly and briefly.

First thou hast scene, says the Lord, a vision,[note 6.7] whereat thou waste amassed: but fear thou not. For thou hast not scene any evil or fearful spirit, boding any misfortune: but my shape, which am thy redemer and Lord. I am first and last. And this manner of speaking (as I warned a little before) he toke out of the Prophecies of Isaiah, as it is to be seen in the xli. xliiii. xlv. and xlviii. chapters. And he signifies him self to be coequal, and of the same substance with the father in all things, very God, eternal, and incoprehenſible. For look what things the father attributeth to himself, the same also doth the son usurp. But there is no order or time certain to be vnderſtade in first and last, but plainly euerlaſtingnes. Therefore Christ here signifies,[note 6.8] that he is very God, egalle and of the same essence with the father from all eternity. As the same is also much confirmed in John 1.5.10.14. and 17. chapter. This fights against the Heretics, which at that time also, as at this day the Seruetanes, deny the eternal deity of Christ the Lord. And thus, when the true God is of us acknoweledged and believed, he may be for our Salvation. If Christ be not very God, he is not our salvation. For I am God, says the verity: And besides me there is no God, no Salvation.

Secondly he says, I am living, and was dead: whereby he signifies that he toke the true humane nature. The which many also at the same time denied: In like case as the[illegible] be some at this day, which do plainly derogate from th[illegible] humanity of Christ. Against all such manner of heresies the Lord him self confesses that he was dead. Whereby it is now manifest, that he is very man, as he is also very God, of the same eſſece with his father in deity, as he is also of the same substance with us in humanity, like unto us in all things, sin excepted. For he toke not the nature of Angles, but the ſede of Abraham. And it behooved in deed than[illegible] the son of man should be incarnate, that both he might di[illegible] and shed blood.[note 6.9] For the Testament in the dead is finally ratified neither is there any remission made without blood she[illegible] ding. The Lord therefore dies, and ſhedeth blood, to the int[illegible] he might give full remiſſio of sins, and confirm the new[illegible] Testament. Yet even he that was thought to be dead, now lives,[note 6.10] and is that same living, who having vanquished death, the iii. day rose again from the dead, and repared life for a[illegible] beleuers, and inspires into them his own very life.

And therefore adds immediately: behold I am living[illegible] world without end. For now Christ dies no more, death[illegible] shall not rule over him. But rather he is the life of all his fait[illegible] full, who in rising again, brought again life: and that life everlasting, induringe I say world without end. As he him self declares more at large. John. 5.6.10. chapter. And the Apostle to the Rom. 4.1. Corinth. 15. and 2. Timoth. 1.

Moreover where many were wot to doubt of this life gotten and repared by Christ, the Lord him self confirms, that he said, by an other, and says. Amen. As though he should say, this is altogether true and undoubted that I say.

Finally he adds,[note 6.11] and I have the keys of Hell and of death. By the which words again he comfort exceedingly, and expresses his power: and declares howe great he is, and what we have of him. Here must we speak by the way of the keep. The ordinary gloss says very well: he that hath the keys of any house, says he, lets in whom he will, and kepeth back whom he will from entering in. Therefore Christ possesses the keys of death and hell, for that whom he will, he delivers from perpetual codemnation of death: And whom he will,[note 6.12] he suffers to remain iuſtely in the same danger of damnation. And verily Isaiah in the 22. speaking of Eliachim, whom he says should be made Judge in the court of Ezechias: I will lay says he the key of the house of David upon his shoulder: which shall open, and no man shall shut: shall shut, and no man shall open. Therefore are the keys put in the Scripture for the charge and government of the house. Eliachim shall govern all thing in the Court of Ezechias uprightly. What soever he shall determine, no man shall infringe, that which he shall abrogate, no man shall restore. Christ therefore, a figure of whom Eliachim represented, shall him self have also the chief government in the house or kingdom of God: so that whom he will, he may quick, and pluck back from hell and from damnation: And again whom he list to condemn, he may diſtroie by his just judgment. For he hath most full power over death and hell.[note 6.13] For both two hath he overcome and made weak. And these things comfort the faithful most ſtrongely, and reteyne them in all Godly duties. And that same is chiefly to be observed, that he says not, he had the keys, or shall have, but I have, says he, I have I say. He gave not his power to the Bishop of Rome, but hath i[illegible] him self, and will keep it still for ever. And he gave not to the Apostles full power of life and death, of salvation and damnation,[note 6.14] and so unarmed him self: but he gave the keys of opening and shutting heaven, as it were to his Ministers and servitors, by the preaching of the Gospel: by the which he promised life to all that believed, Christ him self should give that life for the truth of the promise: To whom so ever they should threaten damnation, Christ him self should condemn for the truth of his word. We see therefore that the Lord kepeth still and exercises the power, and his ministers the ministry (by preaching, not by absolute power.) Therefore the Pope is Antichrist,[note 6.15] which usurps and takes upon him this full power and authority in Heaven and in Earth, and in the midst of the earth also, or beyond all the earth, i[illegible] those unfortunate Ilandes. I mean purgatory. By the which[illegible] crafty devise he hath subtly emptied the purses, Coffer[illegible], garuers and wine cellars,[note 6.16] of foolish people that ſwarne from the articles of their belief, to wit I believe the forgeuenes o[illegible] sins, the resurrection of the flesh, and life everlasting. Th[illegible] beast dare usurp the two horns of the Lamb,[note 6.17] namely th[illegible] authority of King and Bishop, and therefore to hang two key[illegible] under his triple Crown: that even by these Arms all the world may perceive, that this is very he, which, hauin[illegible] subdued three kings or horns, is crop up, and challenges t[illegible] him self all power in heaven and in earth, signified by the tw[illegible] keys. And surely the blindness of our time is wonderful an[illegible] to be lameted, that having eyes it sees yet nothing. Let ſuc[illegible] as be wise, remember, that Christ hath yet the keys of deat[illegible] and Hell, his ministers the denouncing of life and death.

[note 6.18]And now when he had declared these great and moſ[illegible] wholesome matters, and had comforted the mind of John, h[illegible] adds the commandment: write the vision exhibited, f[illegible] nally write those things also which must be done short[illegible] after this. He places in the midst, and those that be: th[illegible] is, which are in deed, and true, and be not false. And these[illegible] things are to get authority to this book, finally to the who[illegible] scripture, which is revealed with like truth of the self sam[illegible] Author. And as John is commanded to write without fear[illegible] so are we commanded to Preach and publish the same boldly though the world be never so mad thereat.

He adds moreover, the exposition that remains, and says: The mystery of the seven stars, &c. The reason seems almost vnperſit. Therefore must we understand this is the mystery or sacrament of the seven Stars and Candelſtickes: that it may be as it were a proposition,[note 6.19] and that the exposition should follow immediately: the seven stars, are seven Messengers, &c. And by Sacrament understand a secret mystery, and the very exposition of the mystery. As if you would ſ[illegible] here is to be noted the goodness of Almighty God,[note 6.20] which declares to us him self the hardest places of the Scripture. Where be they therefore that accuse the Scripture of obſcurenes, and contend that it can not be vnderſtade? let us here mark also the common manner of speaking of the whole scripture: seven stars,[note 6.21] be seven Messengers: The seven lights, are seven Churches. For signs receive the names of the things, although they be, and remain in their own substance, and bee not changed into another. This the very contentiouſe persons do grant also, which in the words of the supper, this is my body, will acknowledge no figurative speech at all.

Stars be called Angles. Angles be Gods Messengers, pastors of churches, so called in the ii. and iii. chapter of Malachie. For God sends preachers as Ambassadors to the people, and wills them to be heard in like case as himself. Luke x. John xiii. Let no man therefore tarry till the Lord him self come down from heaven again, and preach [illegible]nto us. Even now he preaches to us by his Messengers, which preach his, that is to say the word of Christ, if [illegible]ou contemn them, you contemn Christ. Preachers be [illegible]alled stars, by reason of their bright and Heavenly doc [illegible]rine, and for their pureness of life.[note 6.22] Beware therefore you Preachers, that you be not wandering Plants, lest the [illegible]aue no light at all, neither in doctrine, nor conversation of [illegible]fe. For than the shall be likened to stars that fall down [illegible]ut of Heaven, as shall happen here after in this book to the [illegible]alſe teachers.

But those stars are not in the head, or in the fete,[note 6.23] or on [illegible]he back or sides: but in the right hand of Christ. Which [illegible]ing hath in deed a great consolation for the Pastors be in the right hand of God, in Gods protection, neither sh[illegible] any man take them out of his hand. God him self also [illegible] ueth pastors, and furnishes them with necessary good[illegible] of the Church. Therefore is the whole government and gl[illegible] ry his. Wherefore the Apostle says also: he that water[illegible] and plants is nothing, but God that gives increase.

Now as concerning the Candelſtickes, there was o[illegible] verily in the Tabernacle of Moses with seven ſockettes,[note 6.24] [illegible] set in seven Candelles. In Salomons temple were ten ca[illegible] delſtickes. The one represented a figure of Christ: And the [illegible] uen thereupon, and the ten, betokened the universality[illegible] Churches, which are lighted all of the only light Chris[illegible] and have of this one, what light so ever they have. And th[illegible] candelſtickes are of Gold. The mystery whereof Arethas e[illegible] pounding: They are all gold, says he, for the purity a[illegible] preciouſnes of faith lying hid in them. And in deed the [illegible] delſtickes of them selves give no light, but be receptacles [illegible] light. So of us arriſeth no light, but darkness. But in [illegible] that light everlasting, set a light in the candlestick, the lig[illegible] shines: if Christ illumine the Church with faith and [illegible] retie, than faith shows forth her self in open confeſſio[illegible] and the pureness of life in conversation. And this the Lord [illegible] quireth of his church in the v. chapter of Matthew. So let yo[illegible] light shine &c. And the apostle in the ii. to the Philipp. In t[illegible] midst of a froward and crooked natio shine like lights the world.

And hitherto we have handled the consolation of Chri[illegible] and the exposition of that great and celestial vision, when[illegible] we have learned the mysteries of the faith of Christ and of his Church: to the end we should know that Christ is the Lord reignyng in his Church, and applying all things to the salvation of his faithful. That he sends Preachers, teaches by them, and kepeth and defends them. To him be Glory &c.

## ¶ Of the Epistles revealed out of the throne of God from Christ by an Angel, and received and sent of John. Where also a part of the Epistle to the Epheſians is expounded. The seventh Sermon.

Unto the Messenger of the congregation of Epheſus write: These things says he that holds the seven Stars in his righthand, and walk in the midst of the Seven golden Candelſtickes. I know thy works, and thy labour and thy patiece, and [illegible]ow thou canneſt not forbear them which [illegible]re evil: And examineſt them which say they are Apostles, and are not: And haste found [illegible]hem liars: and hast suffered, and hast patience: [illegible]nd for my names sake haste laboured, and [illegible]aſte not fainted. Nevertheless I have some what against thee, because thou haste left [illegible]hy first love.

Your charity hath scene a certain Image of the lord christ,[note 7.1] [illegible]itting on the right hand of the father in glory: yet so, that [illegible] no wise he either forsakes or neglects his Church. Now follows it more fully and plainly howe our Saui [illegible]ur Christ in Heaven executes the office of the high Bishop, and teaches the whole church by his ministers, rebu [illegible]eth, comfort, and reteyneth it in her duty: Finally tour [illegible]eth always away things hurtful, and auaunceth it to greater things. For here follow seven Epistles, to the ſeue Congregations: that is to wit,[note 7.2] unto all the churches in the [illegible]hole world. For this most ample and wholesome doctrine [illegible]aye not be restrained to a few, sins Christ is Biſſhoppe [illegible]niuerſall.[note 7.3]

But great is the autoritie of these epistles. For they are revealed from the throne of God, by the son of Go[illegible] speaking by an Angel, which prescribes what is to b[illegible] written in those Epistles: S. John receives and writes th[illegible] same, through Christ his commandment, and sends the[illegible] to the seven congregations: And verily they aperteyne n[illegible] less unto us, than if now the bearer entering into the churc[illegible] should deliver these letters unto us.

Moreover in these seven Churches is figured unto v[illegible] the nature,[note 7.4] manners, vices, medicines, rebukes, praises of [illegible] Churches in all times, and what soever is wont to chaun[illegible] about them. Then by examples of most excellent mean[illegible] mixed, of Hypocritical also and wicked. And these our Lo[illegible] doth evidently instruct, reprove, rebuke, and blame, praiſ[illegible] correct, move, exhort, comfort, the same he threatens, an[illegible] promises them also joyful things, &c. This is no light [illegible] common example, but of the son of God, the high and mo[illegible] blessed Bishop: teaching us, howe we should deal with [illegible] congregations after the capacity and disposition of every on[illegible]

And not without cause he chouſeth unto him seven th[illegible] most noble cities of Asia. Certain it is, that Asia was of [illegible] first inhabited, and from thence they were dispersed into [illegible] there parts of the world. Certain it is also, that the Deu[illegible] set up his Throne in Asia, and there reigned in men throug[illegible] Idolatry, murder, ambition, avarice, vncleanes, and filth[illegible] pleasures. For the proverb is know, the laughter Iouica[illegible] It is known what the apostle wrote to the Epheſias in th[illegible] iiii. and v. chapter. Therefore wold our Saviour Christ ove[illegible] throw that Throne of the devil, and set up that his Thron[illegible] of rightuouſneſſe and holiness. Therefore goes he firs[illegible] and chiefly to them of Asia, that by their example the whole[illegible] world might be corrected and amended.

[note 7.5]And amongst other cities of Asia and Iouia, Epheſu[illegible] was most famous, called in the old time the light of Aſi[illegible] And amongst the xii. cities of Iouia accounted the princ[illegible] pall.[note 7.6] Neither was there any other richer or more beautifu[illegible] Church scene any where in Asia, than the temple of Diana [illegible] Epheſus. It stood in the midst of the city, a great wond[illegible] of the Greek magnificence, as writes Plinie. This temp[illegible] is said to have ben two hundredth and twenty years in m[illegible] king of all Asia: And set in a fenny ground, that it should feel of no earth quakes nor opening of the earth. The length of [illegible]t was ccccxxv. foot, the breadth ccxx. It had pillars an ho dreth twenty and seven, dedicated of so many kings. Seek the rest out of the epitome of the famous D. Ioachim Vadiane. The apostle S. Paul first illumined this city with the [illegible]eame of the Gospel. Whose epistle to the same city remains, and a plentiful story in the acts of the Apostles. After Paull was executed, John went to Epheſus,[note 7.7] and from thence preached to all Asia: from thence also was he brought to Rome [illegible]o the emperor Domitian: To Epheſus he returned after his exile: and there at length as the ecclesiastical stories do testify, he slept in the Lord.

And before every epistle, much more to that of the Epheſians, is set a commandment, write.[note 7.8] This commandment gives authority to the writynge, so that we may not axe, whether this writing, ought to be credited? and why it should [illegible]e believed? For here is the expres commandment of God, [illegible]nd the divine authority, whereof curiouſely to inquire is [illegible]hought not without cause unlawful. Moses wrote by the [illegible]ommaundement of God. And by the same commandment of God wrote also the prophets and Apostles. Which than are not their wrytinges believed to be Anthenticall? Certes John said truly and wittily: he that knows God, hey [illegible]eth us: he that is not of God, hears us not.1. John.5. Curious questions cease, where the mind of the godly, or of any poor sheep knows the voice of his Lord and Shepherd.

And let no man think, that this epistle being written to one Angel, that is to a Bishop or pastor, appertains nothing to the Church. For to the end of the epistle is added an acclamation. He that hath an ear let him hear what the spirit says to the congregations. Therefore the pastor is named, but the Sheep are not excluded. All degrees and states in the church know, what is said unto them. Ignorauce says: That which is writ to the Romans, cocerneth me nothing. Yet nevertheless it is entitled to the Angel,[note 7.9] to the intent they pastors may be admonished, what the state of the Church is.

The arg[illegible]meant of the first epistle is thus. Christ declares that he rules over his church, that he takes charge and government of the same. Some thing therein he praises and somewhat he blames: In the mean time he exhorts to repentance, threatening grievous things, and promising most ioyefull. And also he applies this epistle to all churches and comunicateth it to all cogregations in the whole worcester[illegible]. But the epistle is exhortatiue, for it instructs the churches exhorts and directs.

And first in deed he shows, who he is, from whom th[illegible] epistle proceeds, that he may give authority thereunto: an[illegible] may declare also that he is the head of his church, the B[illegible] shop, Duke and governor. That part is take of the imag[illegible] set forth in the first chapter. And follows the prophetic[illegible] manner of speaking: This says he which holds the ſeu[illegible] Stars in his righthade. For the Prophets say likewiſ[illegible] Thus says the lord God of Israel, thus says the lor[illegible] of hostess. Thus says the Lord, which brought thee out [illegible] Aegipte, &c. And two especial things he repeteth of the f[illegible] mer description,[note 7.10] whereby he will be known and do us to v[illegible] derſtande, howe he being Lord and Bishop rules and m[illegible] keth in his church. First he affirms that he holds in h[illegible] hand the seven stars. The hand is a token of working, [illegible] protection, or deliuerauce. The stars we have heard to [illegible] the ministers, and the ministry of the word, or the church Therefore Christ holds the ministry in the church, and [illegible] ministers works the salvation of the faithful. After he [illegible] firmeth that he walk, not ſlepyng, or doyng nothing [illegible] the midst of seven golden candelſtickes. In the midst [illegible] said, to the end we should understand, that he gives h[illegible] self indifferently to all men, and rules over all with like [illegible] and government.[note 7.11] Fulwel wrote hereof D. France lamb[illegible] What, says he is to walk or to be in the midst of cong[illegible] gations, but to assist them, keep, instruct, help them, [illegible] by all means to watch over them. For the which cause [illegible] says also in the last of S. Matthew. Behold I am with you [illegible] ways unto the worlds end. Hereof you have a most [illegible] figure in the law: wherein amongst other things which[illegible] apperteined to the ministry of the high priest, he had [illegible] charge of oil and of seven candels for those must he pour[illegible] and ſnafte, and pour in oil, when it wanted. So Chris[illegible] the high and true Bishop hath the charge of the seven candelles, that is to say, of all congregations: and is careful that they want not that oil, which is mentioned in the 44. Psalm. He watches, that they want not the fire and light of the verity. Finally he ſnafteth and pourgeth by faith, what thing so ever hath need to be pourged in them. Thus far he. Which things when they hear, which make the Bishop of Rome head of the church, It is marvel if by and by they understand not their folly and madness. Here the lord adds also, that he knows the works to wit all both good and evil, as well of the Bishop as of his Church. For the Lord knows all things, and is head Bishop of the Catholic or universal Church which also remembers the thoughts of all men in the world at one instant: who sees what is donne, and what is not donne, and what things are needful, nothing escapes him. And such in deed ought he to be, that is head universal of his Church. And this sentence is repeated, I know thy works, in the beginning of every epistle. And verily it is full of comfort, when we hear that Christ knows all our doings. For we believe also that he hath a faithful care of all our matters.

Now this great Bishop comendeth some things in this congregation of Epheſus. For good works in deed are allowed of Christ, and he praises the same, to the end he might give a spur unto such as run in his way. For first he allows the labour and patience both of the Bishop and Church.[note 7.12] Labour comprises thought and care in the way of God, mortifiyng of the flesh, study of good works, but chiefly the cross and persecution, which the story testifies to have ben extreme and cruel in the time of Domitian. And except the persecuted have patience, they can not endure the labour. Holy patience kepeth us in work and holy labour.

But lest that patience should be stretched to those things wherein to be impatient is accounted praise worthy,[note 7.13] he adds the second point that he praises in them, that thou can not bear evil men. And by these evil he means not weaklings, or such as err without maliciouſnes: But the prophet David says also, Psalm. 119. I have hated the wicked, thy law have I loved. What we should do with the weak in the faith, or with them that err of ignorauce, rather than of o[illegible] ſtinate ſtubbernes, the Apostle hath taught us in the 14. [illegible] the Romans. The example of our Saviour. Hath thaugh[illegible] also, bringing again that strayed Sheep upon his ſhulde[illegible] into the ſhepefolde. Therefore the lord speaks here of t[illegible] obstinate, of the deceivers which delight to err the ſelue[illegible] and to draw others with them into errors, no Christ p[illegible] tience bids to bear with such men.

And in the words following he declares of what ſor[illegible] those evil men were. And thou haste examined them whi[illegible] say they be Apostles, and be not, and haste found them[illegible] airs.[note 7.14] Lo he speaks of the false apostles, of whom in ſ. Ioh[illegible] time there was exceadyng great plenty. For they were [illegible] zareans mixing the law with grace, and attributing I[illegible] fication to the law and to our own rightuouſnes. Wh[illegible] the holy and great counsel at Jerusalem condemned, a[illegible] [illegible] pereth in the xv. chapter of the acts of the Apostles. S[illegible] a false Apostle was Hebion. Eusebius mentions in th[illegible] book of the Ecclesiastical story the xxvii. chapter. Here [illegible] was added Cerinthus that heretic not Apostle. There [illegible] more also, whereof some denied the humanity of Chri[illegible] some his deity. Against whom wrote John in his Gos[illegible] and in his Epistle: And Ireney in the first book against h[illegible] tickes. These the Lord denies to be Apostles, or Apoſt[illegible] call: which the Apostles have also denied Acts. 15. And [illegible] wise the Apostle S. John in his epistle canonical: who [illegible] liar, says he, but he that denies Jesus to be Christ? But if such trouble were in Churches whilst the Apoſ[illegible] were yet living,[note 7.15] if there were than so many deceive[illegible] what marvel is it, though in the dregs of the world, wit in this our last time, there be not a few ſoun[illegible] where be they now that wraſte diſſentions and troubles the defence of their error? The Goſpellers them sell[illegible] say they are at diſſention. God is God of concorde, he[illegible] than should I believe that God is among those that [illegible] sent? So might the Sophiſters also have reasoned in th[illegible] [illegible] poſtles time.

And here have we a pefitte way, in what sort the ch[illegible] ches should work, whileſte troublesome persons like f[illegible] Apostles attempt to divide the Church a ſonder.[note 7.16] For such ringeleaders must be tried and examined: And tried must they be after the christen belefe, and doctrine of the Apostles, and inquisition must be made, whether they be Apostles and true men, or false Apostles and liars. When we shall have found them to be false Apostles and liars, and that they go foreward obstinately in their wickedness, they be not to be suffered: as in deed the Epheſians did not vouch safe to bear with such trompers. And we must know that the Pastors ought one way, and the Christen Magistrate another way, and the people the third way not to abide open heretics. For the Pastor not only bears not with them, in dissembling and taking heed to him self of those wolves, but assails them with wholesome doctrine, and re [illegible]ulſeth them from the ſhepefoldes of Christ: But the Magistrate, because he is a christen Magistrate, and by his duty also, not only as a private person but also as a Magistrate, [illegible]ught to serve Christ, he aught also with the sword of [illegible]uſtice to drive away poison from the church, and to pu [illegible]iſhe manifest blasphemies.[note 7.17] And the people are commaun [illegible]ed, neither to hear them, nor receive them, nor to have [illegible]ny thing to do with heretics, and so not to abide them. They may therefore be ashamed of their naughtiness, and [illegible]retence of their perverse patience, which think it no shame [illegible]o maynteyne Heretics, and to flatter the manifest en [illegible]ies of Christ and the Church. Psalm. 15. He is praised, [illegible]hiche makes not much of the wicked: that is to wit, [illegible]n whose sight the wicked man is vile. Therefore is he [illegible]ghtly blamed, who so ever flatters the ungodly. And [illegible] the hatred in deed is rather against wickedness, than a [illegible]aynſte the person of the wicked, which of it self is com [illegible]aunded to be loved. The Devil at this day reyſeth up the [illegible]de heresies of Hebion, Cerinthus, and of others in Terueto a [illegible]paniarde, and in the Anabaptiſtes, Libertines, and other [illegible]onſters, so that the thing it self, and the danger there [illegible] commands us to watch, and to drive away the most [illegible]uell wolves from the holy Church of Christ, which no [illegible]ertheleſſe do set forth nothing more than patience and cha [illegible]tie, for this intent verily that they might be spared, and might unpunished teach what they lift against Christ, a[illegible] work against his church, yea tear it in pieces with th[illegible] wicked teeth.

But when these evil men are not suffered, but impugn[illegible] which seduce and are seduced,[note 7.18] a great conflict arriſe[illegible] whereof again are labours, thoughts, carefulnes, watc[illegible] ynges, injuries to be suffered for the name of Christ, and d[illegible] fence of the verity. For unless we be here diligent and p[illegible] tient, the deceivers over come. But herin did the churc[illegible] of the Epheſians behave them notably well, in so much th[illegible] the Lord now commends exceedingly the magnani[illegible] tie, patience and constancy of the pastor and of his churc[illegible] For neither ought these things to be expounded, that [illegible] should refer them unto that patience, whereby evil me[illegible] are suffered, and permitted to proceed in their malice a[illegible] diſceiptfulneſſe. For so should this place strive against th[illegible] things which went a little before. Which thing the [illegible] mon interpretour minded as it seems to have eschew[illegible] For thus he reads, and thou haste patience, and hast ſuff[illegible] red, where it is in the Greek, and hast suffered and hast p[illegible] tience. He altered the order, and would not set, haste suffer[illegible] before: but haste patience: lest any man should vnd[illegible] stand that they had suffered the false Apostles. But set b[illegible] fore patience and put after sufferance: That we might v[illegible] derſtande that they suffered not evil men, but evil wrought by evil men. So they with patience travailed [illegible] Christ's name, to wit, to be maintained against naugh[illegible] heresies. And he adds, thou hast not fainted, being were[illegible] and broken with labours. For we are taught to ouerco[illegible] through patient constancy, which is rightly called in d[illegible] the accomplishment of every good work.

All and every of these things we must apply unto [illegible] and understand with what things we may now also this day please Christ our redemer, king and Bishop, truly we walk in the same steppes, wherein we see the co[illegible] gregation of the Epheſians to have walked.

It follows what thing he blamed in the same churc[illegible] that they have left their first love. What time they fir[illegible] received the Gospel by S. Paul, and after by John, a[illegible] other godly men, there was scene a great feruentnes in the words and deeds of the faithefull: which thing may be gathered both by the acts of the Apostles, and also by the epistle of S. Paul to the Epheſians. They loved God and their neighbours with a most fervent zeal. They burned in reforming of manners. But in process of time this heate was well couled, and they wared colder in true godliness. This great mischief he rebukes in them, and as follows desires to have it redressed. And here let us note howe not only revolting Idolatry, and other great crimes are imputed to the church, but also if we slack any thing in holy zeal: so that hereof we may learn, how holy and blameles we ought to be before God. Doubtless we can not here excuse our selves before the divine majesty, which were xxx. years paste more ferueter in this congregation, than we be at this day. &c. Our lord God lighten our minds, that we may please him. To whom be glory.

## ¶ The second part of the Epistle to the Epheſians where is spoken of Penance and of the Nicolaites. The Eight Sermon.

Remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent, and do the first works. Or else I will come unto thee shortly, and will remove thy Cadelſticke out of his [illegible]lace, except thou repeat. But this thou hast, [illegible]icauſe thou hate the deeds of the Nicolai [illegible]anes, which deeds I hate also. Let him that [illegible]ath ears, hear what the spirit says to [illegible]he congregations. To him that ouercometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is [illegible]n the midst of the Paradise of God.

[note 8.1]The accusations of our Saviour Christ, which he uses [illegible] gainſte his servants that be sins, tend not to this en[illegible] doubtless, that men overwhelmed with reproaches might b[illegible] ashamed, despair, and perish: but rather that they should am[illegible] be restored and live. Therefore the Lord Jesus to his blameyn[illegible] by and by annexeth an exhortation to repentance, that th[illegible] may be saved: And also describes the true and lawful penau [illegible]

For we heard what thing he rebuked in the cogregati[illegible] of the Epheſians: let us hear now what the lord require[illegible] of the same, and how he seeks to have the error reform[illegible] verily by repentance, to the which he exhorts. For [illegible] have said that the Lord strikes and heals: chiefly in th[illegible] case. Which doctrine surely is proper and perpetual to th[illegible] church of Christ.

He mentions chiefly of three things in this matter, in his counsel or exhortation to amendment.[note 8.2] First he co[illegible] sells or exhorts to remebre, [illegible] from which[illegible] they are fallen: that is to say, with how great love they ha[illegible] burned hitherto, and now howe could they be wared: [illegible] how fortunate and blessed a state they have stand hither[illegible] and now in howe unfortunate and shameful they lie [illegible] For the acknoweledging of the trespass is the beginning repentance,[note 8.3] if being illumined by faith we consider we[illegible] what benefits we have lost, and in what misery we no[illegible] are in. He that weans he has lost nothing, will no[illegible] be moved to make any search or inquisition, he that th[illegible] keth him self to have fallen from no felicity, he will thin[illegible] of nothing how he may be restored. Therefore in amen[illegible] meant of life it behooves that acknoweledgyng and conf[illegible] zion of sins go before, by the which we may lament b[illegible] fore God our poverty and misery. And in deed they fa[illegible] not from felicity, which were never in any felicity. The[illegible] fore say we,[note 8.4] that holy men may fall, and also be reſtor[illegible] by repentance. Than after the acknoweledgyng of our [illegible] ſerie follows repentance: that is the coming again[illegible] our mind, that we go not forth on always like mad[illegible] men and fools, walking in the way of vanity an[illegible] vnrightuouſneſſe.[note 8.5] For repentance is a returning a co[illegible] uerſion and change, whereby we are turned away fro[illegible] evil, and are converted to good, in returning to our wit, and working rightuouſnes.

For last of all follows that, which expounds this repentance. Do the former works: To wit be hot again in love, work the works of faith, which are fruits worthy of repentance. Behold there needs no new laws,[note 8.6] or long disputations concerning amendment. Briefly, do the former works, not of the flesh, but those which thou began when thou first received the Gospel, and waſt borne again in Christ. Finally this is the true reformation, to do the former works of God. Not the latter, which the error of the world hath devised. This is verily, and shall be the true repentance, to wit, the acknowledging of the sin, the conversion unto God and to good, and a turning away from the Devil and from evil, and working of the first good, or Godly rightuouſnes of the faith in Christ. There be many and sundry disputations of repentance and parts of the same, of the contrition of the heart, confession of the mouth, and satisfaction of that work: But like as there is none briefer than this of Jesus Christ, so verily is there none better or more certain.

To these his exhortations and Godly coucelles he adds threatenings most grievous, if happily peril might pearſe,[note 8.7] that love could not ſtyre or move. And he speaks but few words in deed. But he understands a great evil that can not be spoken nor declared. Except thou repent, I will remove thy candelſtick out of his place. The candelſtick, as the lord him self hath expounded it, is the church. Which stands in her place, whyleſt she leans to Christ: and is of Christ preserved, so long as the preaching of the verity is in the same maintained, and is in deed the church of Christ: She is removed out of her place, when she is without the preaching of the truth, and now leans no more upon Christ, nether is of him defended, but is forsaken, and is no longer in deed the church of Christ. This is done of Christ himself, by his [illegible]uſt judgment, what time our vnthanfulnes, and life that can not repent, drives God to depart from us, to relinquish us to our error and darkness, and leave us to diſceauable men, &c. This sense Arethas acknowledges, who: to remove the church, saith he, is, when it is lest bare and destitute of Gods grace: by reason of the which nakedness, it wavers in doubtful perplexity and in storms that are cast to her by wicked men. And verily we se how at this day the church of the Epheſians is removed out of her place, and no longer inioyet[illegible] the wholesome doctrine of Christ nor that it stands upon th[illegible] wholesome rock Jesus Christ[illegible] But is oppressed with the peſ[illegible] ferous doctrine or rather madness of Mahomet, and lies i[illegible] sorrow under the fete of the Turks. We see at this day i[illegible] Germany (more is the pititie) many candelſtickes removed out of their place, not without the great triumph of Satha and loss of souls. Moreover that same is also to be observed in this threatening, that without terror he saith, I will come to thee shortly. For it is a Phrase of speech. Fo[illegible] we say also,[note 8.8] I will come to thee by and by: That is, I will come to revenge and punish, and that perauenture sooner th[illegible] thou lokeſt for. Most certainly, when so ever I chance [illegible] come, I will take punishment of thee. Let no man therfo[illegible] think to escape unpunished in a life that can not repent.

[note 8.9]Again where the Lord repeteth, except thou repent, he plai[illegible] lie testifies, that the bosom of Gods mercy and clemency, i[illegible] ready open, if we do penance, how soever we have offended him before, in the mean time we learn here openly and moſ[illegible] certainly that we can by no counsels or consultations, [illegible] no armies nor policies preuaile one whit on our perils, [illegible] les we repeat. Therefore unless we will have our churches to [illegible] subverted, and given over to be seduced and diſtroied of the devil [illegible] his seducers, let us repeat in time, and receive again the first love.

[note 8.10]Again he comendeth the singular virtue in this cogregat[illegible] especially for that they have hated the doings of the Nicolait[illegible] which God him self also hates. Let us here mark ever[illegible] word. He saith not thou did sly, or eschew and contin[illegible] but thou hast hated. The force of hatred is great, moving [illegible] to persecute, that thou hate.

[note 8.11]Moreover he saith not, thou hast hated the Nicolaita[illegible] but the works of the Nicolaitans. For we ought to hate t[illegible] person of no man for it self, but the vice in the man so the w[illegible] we shall forsake it, we should love the man with all our hart. A[illegible] that must needs be a great evil, which God him self confess[illegible] that he hates. Here all congregations shall understand, that th[illegible] ought also to hate by all means the heresy and abominatio of the Nicolaitans. Albeit that at this day the name be extingwiſhed yet the heresy and abomination of the Nicolaitans remains.

This Nicolas was of Antioche, one of the seven deacos, of whom mention is made in the.6. chap. of the Acts. He is said to have revolted from the purity of faith, as Iudas did: And where he was before a Gentile (for it is said howe he was a Proselyte) he returned in certain things to gentility as a dog to his vomit. The Nicolaitas be also Gnoſtici, and conſortes of Carpocrates, filthy and most wicked people. Clemes excuses this Nicolas somewhat in Eusebius in the.3. book. 29 chap. of the Ecclesiastical history. But that excuse seems not sufficient or just, since all the auncientes with one mouth accuse the same, and namely the very judgement of God in this present and in the Epistles following. Ireneus condemns him of this same place, in the first book against the Valentinians chap. 27. &c. Tertulian in thend of Heret. preſcrip. touches here wittily the facts of the Nicolaitans, and detests the same. Nevertheless he expounds them not, but passes them over. And I know not how wittily Epiphanius hath uttered and declared in order the wicked and abominable acts, neither to be thought nor told, and most beastly filthines, such as hath not been heard of the heresy. 25.26.27. &. 31. &c. Philaſtrius also and S. Austen have touched the Nicolaitans, either of them in their register of heresies. Shamefaſt [illegible]es will not suffer me to recite. It is enough, if we know that same which in the epistle to Pergamum the Lord him self hath expounded, calling the doctrine of Nicolaitans, the doctrine of Balaa the ſouthſaier. But who knows not, what counsel he gave to Balaac king of Moab and of Madian, and how he prostituted fair wenches to the young men of Israel, by whose acquaintance enticed, they both defiled the selves with fornicatio, and did eat also of meates offered up to Idols, being made partakers of Baalpeor. Let him that will read Ioſe [illegible]hus in the iiii. book of Antiquities. cap. 6. And doubtless the [illegible]acrifices of the Nicolaitans seem to differ nothing from the [illegible]ecrettes of Priapus, or Gerecinthia, or the mother of God [illegible]es, and the nightly service of Bacchus. Ireneus signifies [illegible]penly that the Carpocratites, who are called also Gnoſtici, did not abhor images, but paincted and facioned to the ſelue[illegible] the Images of Jesus and Paul, with the Images of certi[illegible] Philosophers: And that the Image of Jesus, as they talk, was made expressly of Pilate, who commanded the face [illegible] Jesus to be painted lively. &c. But howsoever that was, th[illegible] is certain, that thactes of the Nicolaitans were evil report[illegible] of for their fornications and adulteries: And that the Nicol[illegible] tans abſteined not from images, nor from meates offred [illegible] idols.[note 8.12] Against the which error S. Paul wrote also man[illegible] things.

Hereof let us learn to abhor and fly fornication, a[illegible] never to think of restoring the Stews or other places [illegible] whoredom. Fie for shame. Let us learn hereby to keep h[illegible] virginity, single life, and lawful marriages, fly those d[illegible] the Nicolaitans, Let us learn hereby, to keep well our sell[illegible] from Idols, idolatry, and from all strange kinds of w[illegible] ſhippinges. All those God hates.

[note 8.13]And with an acclamation he pearſeth the ears of all me[illegible] moving all to attentiuenes and holy obedience. And apply[illegible] also this doctrine to all times and to all congregation[illegible] the world. He used his wonted speech, repeated so oft in t[illegible] Gospel: He that hath ears to hear, let him hear. Not th[illegible] it is in our strength to hear and obey God. For God pr[illegible] pares our ears, and with his grace frames and draw[illegible] our hearts. And let them to whom the grace of God is g[illegible] ted, beware least through their negligence, vanity, and lig[illegible] nes they lose it not. Let them show such diligence, as G[illegible] in his word requires and prescribes. They that do th[illegible] have ears to hear. He saith therefore, take the heed to wh[illegible] God now speaks, and whose hearts now he ſtyreth a[illegible] moves, that you lose not this grace through your neglig[illegible] be diligent, attentife, and circumspect, ſtyryng up in yo[illegible] selves the gift of God.

[note 8.14]Now also he provokes to dilligece by authority diui[illegible] The spirit of God speaks and reveals these things, [illegible] the spirit of men or of error, for God speaks by his ſ[illegible] rite, which is red to be the spirit both of the father and o[illegible] son. Moreover he applies all and every thing to all co[illegible] gregations, where he saith, what the spirit saith to the co[illegible] gregations, not to the congregation. It is now than manifest, and out of all controversy,[note 8.15] that those seven churches do represent a figure of all churches throughout the whole world, and that all they be instructed in those seven.

Furthermore least any thing should want to the just exhortation unto repentance, to faith and diligence, last he annexeth a most ample promise, and uses an allegorical speech, that it might have the more grace with it.[note 8.16] To them that overcome he promises to give the fruit of the tree of life, planted in the paradise of God. And alludeth to the.2. Chap. of Genesis. And he tranſlateth the sense from earthly things to celestial. The paradise[note 8.17] of God (by the which some understand the church) is that eurlaſting bless and felicity, whereof the Lord spake to the thief, saying: This day shalt thou be with me in paradise. Herein is the tree of life Christ, communicating to us his eternal life: Which we enjoy and have the fruition of, whilst being conveyed into heaven by him and with him we live. Finally this is that Ambrosia or Godly drink, which the heavenly father gives us to drink. But this great and wonderful good chances not to every one, but only to him that ouercometh. For Adam had not overcome, but vanquished had died. If we therefore shall overcome the flesh, the Devil and the world, and that through Christ, we shall live also in the world to come with Christ.

The complutention book hath, which is in the midst of [illegible]he Paradise of my God. And Arethas expounds it,[note 8.18] and [illegible]ayth: Let no man herewith be offended. All humble things [illegible]gre to the dispensation of the incarnation, which was made for our cause, since that he himself in the Gospel saith: [illegible] ascend unto my father, and your father, to my God, and [illegible]o your God. &c.

And thus far hitherto concerning the Epistle of Jesus Christ by John to the Epheſians, and what profit our churches also, and every of us may receive thereof. The Lord lighten the eyes of our mind.

## ¶ The second Epistle of Jesus Christ by John to them of Smyrna is expounded. And is an exhortati [illegible][illegible] to patience, and consolation in afflictions. The ix. Sermon.

ANd unto the Angel of the cogr[illegible] gation of Smyrna write. These[illegible] things saith he that is first an[illegible] the last, which was dead and i[illegible] alive. I know thy works, and t[illegible] bulations and poverty, but thou art rich[illegible] And I know the blasphemy of them which call them selves Jews, and are not: but a[illegible] the congregation of Satan. Fear none [illegible] those things which thou shalt suffer. Behol[illegible] the Devil shall cast some of you into prison[illegible] to tempte you, and you shall have tribulat[illegible] ten days. Be faithful unto the death, and [illegible] will give thee a crown of life. Let him th[illegible] hath ears, hear, what the spirit saith to congregations, he that ouercometh shall n[illegible] be hurt of the second death.

[note 9.1]Jesus Christ from the right hand of the father, throu[illegible] the ministry of an angel by the Apostle and Euange[illegible] S. John, exhorts the congregations of Smyrna, than [illegible] flicted with all kind of evils for the word of God, unto [illegible] feraunce, and comfort the same sighing now under [illegible] cross, promising great things to them that overcome. A[illegible] verily there can not of this manner and in this matter a be[illegible] or briefer exhortation and consolation be found. For in [illegible] wise it is couched of the eternal wisdom of the father, [illegible] unto all times,[note 9.2] and to all that morn under the cross it [illegible] right well agree. For like as Christ at the right hand of [illegible] father is the catholic or universal Bishop: so verily is [illegible] doctrine general, which he him self also applies to all c[illegible] gregations in the end of this Epistle, and in others. And ſ[illegible] he declares that he loves his church, and is present in the same by his power and aid.

And verily it is to be marveled,[note 9.3] that nothing is blamed in this church, since that some fault is found in manner with all others. Therefore was the church of Smyrna right excellent, how be it not without any spirit. For the Lord of his goodness doth not impute unto us small faults (of the which the Prophet speaks, who shall say, my heart is clean? And from my hid sins cleanse me) so that there be a fervent desire or zeal of Godliness in us, and that we be void of great enormities.

First is showed, unto whom this heavenly letter is sent,[note 9.4] to the Pastor of the church of Smyrna, and to the whole flock. For the captain is said to have ſoughten or fled, or to have taken peace, when the whole army together with him hath done this. And the stories bear witness that Policarpus was that same messenger or pastor of the church of Smyrna, ordained of the Apostles the selves,[note 9.5] namely of S. John, Bishop there, and that he lived in the mystery of this congregation lxxxvi. years. For so many he accounts him self before the Lieutenant Herod, what time he was brought to execution. For in the fourth persecution of the church, Aurelius Antoninus, and Aurelius Comodus, being Emperors, he was taken and brought to the governor: And at length for the open and sincere confessing of Christ he was burnt. He had this very much in his mouth: That nothing ought to be received for true, unless it were known to be set forth by the Apostles. Ireneus affirms that when he was a child he saw this old father a man of great years and reverence, in the third book and third Chapter. against heresies, where he tells many things of him besides. As also doth Eusebius in the iiii. book of theccle. history [illegible]he xiiii. and xv. chapters. And S. Hierom in the register of [illegible]he famous writers of the Church. Eusebius in his Chro [illegible]icis, notes that he suffered Martyrdom in the year of [illegible]ur Lord a. C ixx. Whereby it appears that he was [illegible]ade Bishop of Smyrna in the year of our Lord lxxxiiii. [illegible]r there about. For we said even now, that he had been in that ministry lxxxvi. years. And therefore had he been Bishop of Smyrna many years before the setting forth of th[illegible] Apocalypse which was written in the xcvii. year, would[illegible] God all Pastors would set before their eyes this good Policarpus to be followed, of whom there remains a not[illegible] ble Epistle to the Philippians.

After again is the author of the Epistle[note 9.6] declared, which[illegible] is set forth with two titles, taken out of the former viſi[illegible] of John and deſcriptio of Christ. Thus saith the first and [illegible] last. &c. Whereby is signified the eternal divinity of Christ[illegible] which wants beginning and ending. And of him self is [illegible] uerlaſting. There is added, that he was dead, and lives again[illegible] that is to wit, hath risen from the dead. And this begi[illegible] ning accords right well to the matter. For they perceiu[illegible] that whosoever are afflicted for Christ and his Gospel of m[illegible] mighty kings and princes, have a Lord and patron m[illegible] mighty and more faithful, which in no wise can be ou[illegible] come. Who can also in death keep his, like as he raised [illegible] Christ from the dead, to the intent we might have an op[illegible] testimony, that we shall live with Christ, even in death it se[illegible]

[note 9.7]And now comes he to the matter it self, and the which[illegible] thing he repeteth in all Epistles, he saith here also: I kno[illegible] thy works, to wit both good and evil. Think not that [illegible] neither know nor care for thy matters. Thou art verily to[illegible] ten out in my hands, I know, see and care for thee and [illegible] thine. And these things boeth provoke us marvelously virtue, when we know that we have God a loker on, [illegible] hath a care of us: And also comfort us greatly, which vnd[illegible] stand, how he that loves us, and in no matter neglect[illegible] hath us always as it were before his eyes.

And here particularly he declares what he knew: A[illegible] first in deed the afflictions,[note 9.8] which verily they suffered in t[illegible] present persecution of the Emperor Domitian. And aff[illegible] tion is as it were a general word, to the four kinds fo[illegible] wyng. For he rehearses, touching their substance the ſ[illegible] ling of their goods, and their poverty: In their name [illegible] estimation, cotumelies, reproaches, or blasphemies: In by[illegible] imprisonment, and bonds, yea and death also. For [illegible] these afflictions Godly men are exercised, for the truth sa[illegible] of the wicked. And in these may be copryſed all other kinds of tribulation. The which the Epistle of Jesus Christ recites in a Godly order. There is nothing therefore of these matters which the Lord Christ knows not.

Poverty hath the first place.[note 9.9] Neither ought we to take it here spiritually for the modesty and humbleness of mind: although it be certain, that the church of Smyrna wanted not the same virtue: But there was poverty and lack of all things by reason of the spoiling of their goods. For in time of persecution, by virtue of kings proclamatios, the goods of the faithful professors of Christ are cofiſcated to the kings use, or permitted to the soldiers, nobles, or promoters to take at their pleasure: The faithful thrust out of their houses, are either driven into exile, or go a begging: would God we wanted examples thereof at this day. Let us hereof learn to bear and suffer patiently the like chances also, being persuaded that God knows our necessity. And because it is an hard thing [illegible]or an honest man to honger and want with his family, for a [illegible]omfort and consolation he adds, but thou art rich.

This to the world seems a paradoxe, or incredible.[note 9.10] What will they say, is he rich that hath nothing, and is brought [illegible]o the state of beggars? There be doubtless goods and ry [illegible]hes of the mind much better than corporal substance.

For this may be had, without the true felicity, of rich men [illegible]f this world, that live a most miserable life. Again you [illegible]hall see a poor man, concerning worldly goods, but fur [illegible]iſhed with the rycheſſe of the mind, for this cause only to [illegible]e happy and most blessed. He covets nothing, he is con [illegible]ent with his vocation: Neither would he change his state with most wealthy and rich kings. Contrariwyſe you [illegible]hall see rich men but of an evil conscience, and therefore [illegible]houghtfull and burdened with cares, and never merry. You [illegible]hall see poor men, but with merry hearts to lead a joyful [illegible]fe. Why than should it seem marvel, if he that is ſpoy [illegible]ed of his worldly goods for Christ, and enriched with the [illegible]iftes of the mind, is glad and rejoices in God, and takes a good part all chances, and for the same cause is judged [illegible]o be verily rich? Doubtless the wise men of this world [illegible]we also, that the only wise man is trewly rich. Which is gallantly discoursed of Cicero. Arethas saith, in spiritual matters having a treasure hid in the field of thy heart, which is Christ, by reason of who thou art rich also: Sin[illegible] thou hast him thy protector, who also when he was rich, for us became poor. &c.

[note 9.11]In the second place is recited blasphemy, by the which[illegible] we understand all manner of railings and ſclauders, when[illegible] by the name and estimation of the faithful is hurt. Of th[illegible] which sort are these: They be heretics and Schismatic[illegible] so many as be favourers of this religion: They be wicke[illegible] people, diſpiſers of God and his saints, the ennemies of [illegible] Gods service, and therefore the plagues of the common wealt[illegible] which if they be suffered, the common wealth must needs [illegible] destroyed. And these things in deed many times vex go[illegible] men more grievously, than the loss of their goods. For w[illegible] will not set more by a good name, than by great rich[illegible] Therefore the Lord in Gospel of S. Math. the.10. Chapter[illegible] With many words heals this disease: And exhorts [illegible] that for the avoiding of that infamy, they should commit[illegible] thing unworthy the name of christians.

[note 9.12]In the mean time he declares also, what moved the [illegible] thours of this mischief, whom he blames also exceadyn[illegible] to the intent that the godly should understand, how greatly th[illegible] ennemies of all Godliness are of God miſliked: And the[illegible] might also care the less for their hatred and perſecutio. T[illegible] say in deed, that they be Jews, where they be nothing [illegible] Thus also S. Paul handled the Jews in the ii. to the [illegible] mains. The Jews are called confessors, honorers, and faithful servants of God. But these blaspheme Gods na[illegible] they impugn the true faith, and oppress them that prof[illegible] and worship God. Therefore be they not Jews. W[illegible] than? The Synagoge, congregation or aſſemblee of Sa[illegible] Thus the very son of God plucks of the viſerne fr[illegible] these varlettes, to the comfort of all those that suffer pe[illegible] cution, of them that set forth them selves with stout titles the end it should never grieve them, that they are condemn[illegible] of such harlottes, the children of the Devil. Christ att[illegible] teeth to them the true title, and calls them not the o[illegible] holy, and catholic Church of God, but the conspire[illegible] and school of Satan, as in whom, not the spirit of God, but of Satan, inspires lies, iuggelinges, diſceiptes, blasphemies, fires and deaths. Therefore let it not grieve thee at this day, [illegible]n case it be thy fortune to be condemned for the Gospel, of those that call them selves most holy, most shining, most reverent, and most irreprehenſible Prelates and Patrones of the old church, religion, and catholic faith, which have on their side, counsels, fathers, so many successions of Biſhoppes, the prescript of so long time, and consent of so many Realms. They be nothing less than that they desire to be called: But rather the champions of Antichrist, and the professed ennemies, and treaders under feet of all christen piety. For whom is prepared everlasting destruction.

After this he puts an exhortation and a consolation most evident, before the which setting also the son,[note 9.13] he saith, fear nothing of all that thou shalt suffer. The son of God him self feared the cross and death, and it is a natural thing to fear evils, and death. Therefore we are not commanded, that we should non be men, and that like [illegible]toickes we should say howe the same things grieve us not, which nevertheless tourment us exceedingly: but [illegible]he faithful are encouraged, that they should stand strong [illegible]n the faith, neither that they should doe any thing vnwor [illegible]hy the same for fear of punishment. We be therefore com [illegible]aunded boldly and cherely to contemn or suppress fear, [illegible]nd to crave strength by the spirit of God, and to exercise it [illegible] temptations.

There follow reasons,[note 9.14] whereby he may obtain that he [illegible]ath persuaded, may confirm, comfort and exhort them [illegible] patience and constancy. He prophecies therefore to the Godly, what thing they shall suffer: And touches also the [illegible]hird kind of affliction, imprisonment and bonds, under [illegible]hiche we understand all puniſhementes whereby our by [illegible]ies are tourmented. But to be warned before of the evil, is great benefit. We are more easily overcome of vnproui [illegible]ed perils. And therefore the Lord in the Gospel after S. [illegible]ath. the x. Chapter. and after John in the xv. and xvi. Chapters [illegible]lleth his disciples of many evils that should come unto them, and adds thereto: These things have I spoken to you, that when the time shall come, the might remembr[illegible] them, that I have told you before. So now also faithfully warns the faithful in this Epistle.

[note 9.15]And he touches the author of these evils, saying: Th[illegible] Devil will cast some of you into prison. Therefore we perceive that those euillies arise of the common ennemy of mankynde, and of the salvation of the faithful: Whereof we may conjecture, that he goes about to intercept our salvation, and that we ought therefore to stand more earſtly against him. The soldiers when they hear that their old ennemy is at hand, wax not ſluggyſhe, but cherefull. But the Devil inspires evil men, corrupts Princes and Mag[illegible] ſtrates, which attempt persecution against the church. S[illegible] we read that Satan afflicted Iob, that is to have prou[illegible] ked the Chaldeis and Sabeis to kill his ſeruautes and dri[illegible] away his cattle. Here therefore they may see, with what ſ[illegible] rite they are encouraged, which at this day persecute t[illegible] church of Christ, for the profession of the verity. The Go[illegible] lie have that which may comfort them: For they hear th[illegible] the same filthy beast is set against them, which so oft be[illegible] vanquished of Christ the Prince of the faithful, and of t[illegible] faithful through Christ's aid, may without any difficul[illegible] be overcome. And verily the Lord permits to the De[illegible] and deuelyſhemen power over his servants. If thou m[illegible] uell why,[note 9.16] hear: That you may be tempted. God permit[illegible] not his to Satan, that they should peryſhe, but that t[illegible] should be tempted and tried. Therefore to a good end are [illegible] delivered to the fire, that we might be pourged from our thines, that the virtue of our faith mought shine, and G[illegible] might be glorified, and we made the purer. Who therf[illegible] will hereafter be impatient, when we hear that we for t[illegible] great good are put to evil? We read in the iii. of Wiſdo[illegible] As gold is tried in the fire, so are the faithful proved. T[illegible] parable hath S. Peter expounded at large in the iiii. Cha[illegible] of the first Epistle.[note 9.17] Where he that will may have it mor[illegible] boundauntly.

Moreover the time also of tribulation is assigned [illegible] that for ten days. The tenth number signifies a multitu[illegible] For Iacob says to his father in Law: Ten times haste thou changed my wages. Genesis xxxi. and Numeri xiiii. He saith he was tempted ten times, that is often, and many times. Iob also affirms him self in the xxix. chapter. To have been injuried ten times. The Lord therefore saith at this present. You shall be diversely and much molested with evils. Notwithstanding forasmuch as he puts not months, years, nor ages, but days, he prophecies that the evils shall not be continual, but that there shall always be spaces be twirte to breathe in, verily for the shortness of persecution, first Essay the.26. Chapter. Secondly S. Peter in the first. Petri. 1. Do comfort the faithful. It is the part of the faithful not to prescribe God: But whether we be put to payne a long time or short, to take it patiently. Let us think rather, that in the long continuance of evils, there is some end also forſene of the Lord: And that in the same time of breathing, we must repare the evils, and return unto battle.

Last,[note 9.18] are the Godly encouraged by a most ample and large promise, in the which is also included the fourth and most grievous kind of affliction, also the bitter death it self, through fire, halter, sword, water. &c. But in case thou be not afraid of death, but vanquishing it also, shall offer up thy self unto God, than will I give thee, saith the Lord, a crown of life. Hereunto is annexed the state of the Epistle, and some of all. Therefore be thou faithful, cherefull, constant, even to the very death, For the Lord saith also in the Gospel: Who so perseveres to the end, he shall be saved. And we read that the Apoſtel hath said, if we die with Christ, we shall live with him. And truly the crown of life is none other thing, than eternal life, and that everlasting, celestial and unspeakable joy. And the Lord alluded to conflicts, after the which luckily finished, the victors are crowned. Blessed is the man, saith the Apostle S. James, that suffers temptation: because that when he shall be tried, he shall receive a crown of life, which the Lord hath promised to those of whom he is loved. Like things hath the Apostle S. Paul written also in the first to the Corinthians the ix. and in the second to Timothee the iiii. Chapter. Therefore let it be harde hereafter for no man to lose this temporal life. Where[illegible] as the same being lost for Christ, we shall receive eternal life and otherwise will we nyll we must die. Let us therefore b[illegible] content rather to die blessedly, than to live miserably, so th[illegible] we see we may so please God.

Finally like as in the end of the first Epistle, he comm[illegible] nicated and applied the same wholly to all times and chu[illegible] ches,[note 9.19] least any should suppose that these things concern[illegible] him nothing: So in the end of this Epistle also, he both pre[illegible] cheth the spirit to be author of all these things, and exho[illegible] teeth all men to hear and obey diligently, and affirms thy[illegible] to be written unto all congregations in the world for edifying. Moreover the promise of life he communicates to [illegible] saying: He that ouercometh shall not be hurt of the second death. This is spoken to all men and women, if thou overcome[illegible] Therefore must we overcome the world, the Devil, the flesh[illegible] and all temptation. And we must overcome by him, which faith, by his spirit, dwelling in us: And that we should wall[illegible] that way, wherein he hath commanded us to walk. If tho[illegible] overcome,[note 9.20] thou shalt not be hurt in the second death. Th[illegible] mas of Aquine saith. That the first death is of sin, the ſeco [illegible] of pain. We understand plainly by the first death the na[illegible] rall separation of the soul from the body, which also co[illegible] meth to us for sin, as appears in the iii. of Genesis. Th[illegible] same is come to good and evil. For we be all earth, and [illegible] to earth we shall return. And by and by follows the [illegible] cond death and the second life: They that believe in Christ, overcome, feel nothing of the second death, but live, as t[illegible] Lord him self assures us in the iii. and v. Chapter of Ioh[illegible] He shall not come into judgment, but hath passed fro dea[illegible] to life.
